texts.orig: Post-combustion carbon dioxide capture presents tremendous challenges from a chemical engineering perspective. In the first generation of industrial installations, gas-liquid absorption using chemical solvents in packed columns is classically considered to be the best technology available [1]. However, the following two major challenges must be met to achieve the technical and economical targets: first, the decrease of the energy requirement of the process through novel solvents or heat-integration approaches, and second, the decrease of the size of the installation through process intensification.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A generic one-dimensional model was developed, leading to a better comprehension of the intensification potential of hollow fiber membrane contactors for gas absorption in a chemical solvent. The methodology was applied to an illustrative case study, namely the chemical absorption of CO2 in industrially relevant operating conditions for post-combustion capture. These conditions introduce numerous constraints, thereby leading to an estimate of the dimensions of the hollow fiber membrane contactor as a function of its geometrical characteristics. In the parametric range that was considered, the flow regime is laminar, and the concentration profiles are fully developed both for the gas and liquid flows that lead to masstransfer coefficients that are independent of the flow velocities. The influence of the external fiber radius and the membrane permeability and thickness on overall process performance are clearly evident. Liquid flow around the fibers that proceeds with gas flowing through the lumen of the fibers is shown to be particularly effective. Under favorable conditions (i.e., an external fiber radius of 2 × 10 -4 m and sufficient membrane permeability), the overall contactor volume can be divided by a factor of approximately twenty relative to the volume of an absorption column. Under these conditions, the superficial gas velocity can be maintained at a high level (i.e., greater than 1 m s -1 ) with a drop in gas and liquid pressure of only approximately 50 mbar.

texts.orig: this role is played by the absorption liquid. The specific interfacial area for mass transfer in a membrane gas-liquid contactor is the membrane surface area per contactor volume. For commercially available hollow fiber membrane modules, this ranges from 1500 to 3000 m -1 , depending on the diameter and packing density of the hollow fibers [2]. This area is much higher than the specific contact areas that are available in conventional packed columns used for chemical absorption of CO2, which are approximately 230 m -1 in typical situations [3], and this increased area can potentially lead to significantly higher specific absorption flux. This confers tremendous advantages, particularly in offshore applications, in which weight and volume considerations play a key role. Another interesting feature of hollow fiber membrane contactors lies in the physical separation of the gas and liquid phases, thereby avoiding the flooding and solvent entrainment that can occur in packed columns.

texts.orig: Process models of various complexities have been developed [6]. The simplest models define an average overall mass-transfer coefficient. The principal assumptions of these models are the invariance of the gas-side, the membrane and the liquid-side conductances over the entire fiber length. Although these models can be valid for particular operation conditions (e.g., a high excess of liquidside reactant in the process), they cannot be applied to industrial operating conditions in which the liquid-side conductance varies widely over the reactor length as discussed below. Recently, several two-dimensional models of various complexities that are based on the resolution of the convective diffusion equations coupled to the equations for the chemical reaction in a cylindrical geometry were proposed [8-12]. In general, these models were used to determine select parameters in particular situations such as the effective membrane mass-transfer coefficient. Nevertheless, complex two-dimensional approaches do not provide the opportunity to achieve a systematic parameter sensitivity analysis or to identify the optimum industrially relevant operating parameters. This study intends to fill the gap between extremely simple and highly complex models with a one-dimensional engineering-type model of somewhat intermediate complexity. One-dimensional models that are based on series resistance approaches have been already proposed for interpreting membrane contactors [2,4,5]; however, to the best of our knowledge, no research to date has performed a systematic analysis of the combined influence of the membrane, the module and the operating conditions on the overall gas absorption performance of a membrane contactor. Here, we report a generic one-dimensional modeling approach of a membrane contactor for gas absorption in a chemical solvent. The model predictions are then used to determine and optimize the most important process parameters using a case study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper as with the vast majority of reports that are published regarding post-combustion CO2 capture has deliberately focused on CO2 absorption in monoethanolamine (MEA). It should be noted, however, that MEA has several shortcomings in carbon dioxide capture applications. Consequently, it is not certain whether MEA will ultimately be selected for CO2 scrubbing of post-combustion flue gases at an industrial scale. Nevertheless, the methodology that is proposed in this paper can be easily adapted to any absorption process in a chemical solvent. Changing the solvent will obviously quantitatively modify the results and the conclusions in terms of the performance of membrane contactors for process intensification. This point will be investigated in future studies.

texts.orig: The paper is structured as follows. Some basic model assumptions as well as the parametric domain are exposed in Section 2. The hydrodynamics of the gas and the liquid flow are discussed in Section 3, thus leading to the choice of the most relevant masstransfer relations. Assumptions that relate to the modeling of the reactive diffusion in the liquid-side boundary layer are discussed in Section 4. In Section 5, the variation in the specific local molar flux of the gaseous reactant is discussed in detail. Finally, in Section 6, the global characteristics of the hollow fiber membrane contactor are derived and discussed.

texts.orig: The basic model assumptions are listed in Table 1. Hydrophobic porous membranes with uniform pore size and wetting characteristics are considered. Similar to previous studies of membrane contactors, the system is presumed to be isothermal, at steady state and to have perfect counter-current plug flow of the gas and the liquid phases. Two flow configurations are evaluated and compared: liquid flowing through the fibers (a so-called 'liquid-in' configuration) or liquid flowing around the fibers (a so-called 'liquid-out' configuration).

texts.orig: For given operating conditions, the volumetric liquid flux is considered to be invariant over the length of the membrane contactor, whereas the volumetric gas flux varies slightly between the inlet and the outlet sections; this is due to the gas-phase pressure drop on one hand, which leads to an increase in volumetric gas flux, and the transfer of CO2 from the gas to the liquid phase on the other hand, which leads to a decrease in volumetric gas flux. For the investigated operation constraints (i.e., the gas-phase pressure drop and the CO2 capture ratio), the resulting overall variation in volumetric gas flux between the inlet and outlet section is approximately 10%.

texts.orig: The performance of the membrane contactor depends on the specific interfacial area, which is defined by the geometric characteristics of the fiber bundle as shown in Table 2. The specific interfacial area is proportional to both the fiber volume fraction of the membrane module and the reciprocal of the fiber radius. High fiber volume fractions (i.e., ϕ > 0.5) and small fiber radii (i.e., re < 10 -3 m) are most relevant for commercial purposes. The specific interfacial area that is explored in the present paper ranges from 1200 to 6000 m -1 when based on the external membrane area. Important relative membrane thicknesses lead to a decrease in the specific internal and membrane areas but do not affect the specific external area.

texts.orig: The membrane mass-transfer characteristics are expressed using the membrane permeability concept. This utilizes the key variable ε / /FS in which ε represents the membrane material porosity and /FS represents the tortuosity. These parameters will clearly influence to a large extent the impact of the membrane on the overall mass-transfer coefficient, thus ultimately controlling the intensification effect. A range of ε / /FS between 0.2 and 0.001 was investigated. When combined with the membrane thickness and gas diffusion coefficient in the pores, this corresponds to a membrane mass-transfer coefficient ( km ) that ranges from 2 × 10 -2 to 8 × 10 -5 m s -1 . Previously reported data regarding membrane mass-transfer coefficients are consistent with this range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To create sufficiently large specific surface areas, the fiber radius is typically in the millimeter or submillimeter range. Therefore, in typical operating conditions, the Reynolds numbers are sufficiently small to maintain a laminar flow regime. In laminar flow (and for low solute concentrations and transfer rates), the mass and the heat transfer processes are generally treated by analogy [21,22]. In laminar flow through a cylindrical pipe, the convective heat and mass-transfer coefficients can thus be estimated by integrating the Graetz equation for suitable boundary conditions [21-23]. The two dimensionless numbers characterizing the resulting mass-transfer relation are the Sherwood and the Graetz number, denoted Sh and Gz respectively. For low Graetz numbers, at the pipe entrance, the convective-diffusion boundary layer is under development, and the local Sherwood number decreases with increasing distance from the pipe entrance. For sufficiently high Graetz numbers (i.e., Gz > 0.03), the radial concentration profile is fully developed, and the local Sherwood number attains a limiting value. Under these conditions, the mass-transfer coefficient is solely a function of the diffusion coefficient and the hydraulic flow diameter and is independent of the flow velocity. Fully developed concentration profiles are often encountered in submillimeter systems, as the diffusion distances are small.

texts.orig: In the following sections, laminar flow (as well as fully developed concentration profiles for both the gas-side and the liquid-side flows) is assumed over the entire fiber length. Because the variation in the local fluxes is less important than the variation in the interfacial concentrations (see Section 6), the limiting Sherwood number that is considered is that which corresponds to uniform mass flux (i.e., 4.36) (see Table 4). It should be emphasized that the assumption of developed concentration profiles in laminar flow leads to cautious estimates of the local external and internal mass-transfer coefficients and implies sufficiently low Reynolds numbers and sufficiently high material Graetz numbers. The validity of these assumptions is checked in Appendix B for the investigated geometrical and operational ranges.
texts.text: In the following sections, laminar flow (as well as fully developed concentration profiles for both the gas-side and the liquid-side flows) is assumed over the entire fiber length. Because the variation in the local fluxes is less important than the variation in the interfacial concentrations (see Section 6), the limiting Sherwood number that is considered is that which corresponds to uniform mass flux (i.e., 4.36) (see Table 4). It should be emphasized that the assumption of developed concentration profiles in laminar flow leads to cautious estimates of the local external and internal mass-transfer coefficients and implies sufficiently low Reynolds numbers and sufficiently high material Graetz numbers. The validity of these assumptions is checked in Appendix B for the investigated geometrical and operational ranges.

texts.orig: The Hatta number, Ha , compares the maximum material fluxes with and without a reaction in a case in which the diffusion of the liquid-phase co-reactant (i.e., free MEA in the investigated system) is not limiting. Fig. 2 illustrates the variation in the Hatta number for the liquid-in configuration given the geometric characteristics of the fiber bundle. Due to the important depletion of the liquidphase reactant (see Table 3), the Hatta number is always higher in the inlet section of the liquid flow ( z = Z ), where it reaches values of approximately 1000 for a fiber radius of 10 -3 m; this is in contrast with the outlet section ( z = 0), in which the values attained are divided by a factor of approximately four. Furthermore, the Hatta number decreases as the liquid flow section decreases, as in this case, the mass-transfer coefficient increases. Nonetheless, under all investigated conditions, the Hatta number is higher than 50, thus confining the reaction to a thin film that is close to the membrane surface. This holds true for the liquid-out configuration (data not shown). Due to the elevated Hatta numbers, curvature effects in the actual reaction layer can be neglected, and the well-documented model of the competition between reaction and diffusion in a plane geometry [21] should be sufficient to describe the phenomena that occur in the membrane contactor. Similar conclusions were previously proposed in the literature [2]. This model is thus applied in the present work.

texts.orig: The second dimensionless number that is used in reactive diffusion of a bimolecular reaction is the limiting enhancement factor, E lim , the definition of which depends on the assumptions that are made on the mass-transfer boundary layer. Because the masstransfer boundary layer in the liquid phase is considered in this work to be fully developed, the mass-transfer coefficient, kL-j , is proportional to the diffusion coefficient of the considered species, thus leading to the expression of the limiting enhancement factor that is reported in Table 5a [21]. This limiting enhancement factor essentially compares the material fluxes of the two reactants in the liquid phase. In the case of fast reactions or low concentrations of the liquid-phase co-reactant, the limiting enhancement factor controls the overall reactive absorption flux.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Knowing the overall mass-transfer coefficient, Kov , allows one to estimate the local molar flux of the gaseous reactant. Fig. 7 illustrates the variation in the local molar flux of the gaseous reactant at the inlet and outlet sections of the membrane contactor for the liquid-in configuration. At the gas-inlet section ( z = 0), the local molar reactant flux is limited by the liquid-side mass-transfer resistance and is thus independent of membrane permeability and relative thickness. The local molar reactant flux depends on the fiber radius, as small-radius fibers lead to a high interfacial area (see Table 1). Because of the important liquid-side mass-transfer enhancement, local molar reactant fluxes may be higher in the gas-outlet section, where the reactant concentration in the gas phase is low ( z = Z ) than in the gas-inlet section ( z = 0), where the reactant concentration in the gas phase is high. At this location, the membrane resistance (combining permeability and thickness) influences the local reactant flux; thus, thin and permeable membranes lead to higher fluxes. Similar conclusions can be drawn for the liquid-out configuration (data not shown).

texts.orig: 6.2. Reactor performance for an infinitely high gas-and-membrane mass-transfer coefficient
texts.text: 6.2. Reactor performance for an infinitely high gas-and-membrane mass-transfer coefficient

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To estimate the optimal performance of the membrane contactor as a function of its geometric characteristics, simulations were performed for an infinitely high gas-and-membrane mass-transfer conductance. In this case, the gas-phase reactant concentration at the gas-liquid interface equals the gas-core reactant concentration in the considered reactor section. The reactor characteristics that are discussed are solely related to the reaction kinetics, the gas-liquid interfacial area and the liquid-side mass-transfer limitations. The simulations were performed for the operational parameters and constraints that are given in Table 3. The simulation results were used to validate the assumptions that were made regarding hydrodynamics and mass transfer, as discussed in Appendix B.

The dimensionless conductance decreases from the liquid-inlet section ( z = Z ) where it is close to unity to the liquid-outlet section ( z = 0). The decrease is due to the consumption of free MEA in the liquid phase, thus leading to an increase in mass-transfer limitations. Because the mass-transfer coefficient is proportional to the reciprocal of the hydraulic flow diameter of the liquid-side flow section (see Table 4), mass-transfer limitations are more pronounced with a fiber radius of 10 -3 m (Fig. 9b) relative to a fiber radius of 2 × 10 -4 m (Fig. 9a). For the liquid-in configuration, the hydraulic flow diameter of the liquid flow decreases with the relative membrane thickness, and higher dimensionless overall conductances are therefore observed for high relative membrane thicknesses. For the liquid-out configuration, the dimensionless overall conductance is not dependent on relative membrane thickness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The fiber radius is clearly the primary parameter with respect to process intensification issues. For example, with a fiber radius of 2 × 10 -4 m, the average specific molar reactant flux reaches 26.9 mol m 3 s -1 in the liquid-out configuration, which represents an impressive process intensification factor compared to a flux of 1 mol m 3 s -1 that is typically achieved in absorption columns [3]. On the contrary, a fiber radius of 10 -3 m does not lead to significant process intensification, as the average specific molar reactant fluxes that are achieved in this configuration range from 1 to 1.67 mol m 3 s -1 . This result highlights the key role that is played by material constraints in the preparation of small-diameter fibers. For example, the difficulties associated with polytetrafluoroethylene PTFE properties make the production of low diameter fibers difficult. The results of the present study as shown in Fig. 11 permit an evaluation of the impact of these preparation limitations on intensification performance.

texts.orig: 6.4.1. Material constraints: chemical stability and breakthrough pressure
texts.text: 6.4.1. Material constraints: chemical stability and breakthrough pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the preceding sections, it is presumed that the membrane material exists and is stable under absorption process conditions. In practice, the long-term stability of membrane materials is an ongoing research issue for many absorption systems, including aqueous amine solutions [6]. Considering the latter, the sensitivity of polymers to both moderate alkaline solutions and surface oxidation must be considered, as alkyl amine degradation generates side products that can induce significant modifications in the polymer surface's properties [28]. Polypropylene (PP) and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) are often considered in this framework, and PTFE appears to be particularly suitable due to its higher longtime stability [29].

texts.orig: The relative membrane conductance of microporous membranes depends on two main parameters, namely porosity and tortuosity, both of which depend on the membrane preparation procedure. Attainable upper limits of membrane porosity are due to the mechanical fiber's properties, which deteriorate for high porosities, but are also due to the increase in the maximum pore size that is observed for high porosities, thus leading to a decrease in the system's breakthrough pressure. Clearly, a compromise between permeability and structure must be reached.

For example, the space-averaged specific molar flux of carbon dioxide can exceed 20 mol m -3 s -1 with a fiber radius of 2 × 10 -4 m, whereas it is only approximately 1.5 mol m -3 s -1 with a fiber radius of 10 -3 m. texts.orig: Appendix A. Estimation of the fluid-solid mass-transfer coefficient in developed laminar axial flow through an array of cylinders
texts.text: Appendix A. Estimation of the fluid-solid mass-transfer coefficient in developed laminar axial flow through an array of cylinders

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The physical mass-transfer process in hollow fiber membrane modules has been discussed in detail in the literature [4]. An overview of available mass-transfer correlations is given in reference [26]. The mass transfer for the liquid flowing around the fibers is actually a three-dimensional problem, as the interaction of the various fibers should be taken into account. A two-dimensional approximation that is suggested frequently is flow through an annulus that is characterized by the hydraulic diameter of the external flow section [33]. Numerical solutions for the three-dimensional formulation were presented for heat transfer in laminar flow for a fluid flowing axially between regular arrays of cylinders with a uniform surface temperature [34] or with a uniform wall heat flux [35]. The numerical results clearly confirm the existence of a limiting (or asymptotic) Nusselt number for sufficiently high thermal Graetz numbers. The analogy between heat and mass transfer that is generally stated for sufficiently low solute concentrations and transfer rates [22] permits one to exploit these results for estimating the limiting Sherwood numbers that one would obtain in laminar mass transfer with a uniform mass flux or uniform wall concentrations in the same geometry.
texts.text: The physical mass-transfer process in hollow fiber membrane modules has been discussed in detail in the literature [4]. An overview of available mass-transfer correlations is given in reference [26]. The mass transfer for the liquid flowing around the fibers is actually a three-dimensional problem, as the interaction of the various fibers should be taken into account. A two-dimensional approximation that is suggested frequently is flow through an annulus that is characterized by the hydraulic diameter of the external flow section [33]. Numerical solutions for the three-dimensional formulation were presented for heat transfer in laminar flow for a fluid flowing axially between regular arrays of cylinders with a uniform surface temperature [34] or with a uniform wall heat flux [35]. The numerical results clearly confirm the existence of a limiting (or asymptotic) Nusselt number for sufficiently high thermal Graetz numbers. The analogy between heat and mass transfer that is generally stated for sufficiently low solute concentrations and transfer rates [22] permits one to exploit these results for estimating the limiting Sherwood numbers that one would obtain in laminar mass transfer with a uniform mass flux or uniform wall concentrations in the same geometry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. A1 depicts the variation in the resulting limiting Sherwood number that is obtained by numerical simulation as a function of the cylinder volume fraction for regular arrays of cylinders [34,35]. The two investigated limiting conditions the uniform wall concentration (temperature in the original paper) and the uniform flux to the wall are shown. The Sherwood number is defined in Fig. A1 with respect to the hydraulic flow diameter of the external flow section (see Table 2), which differs from the original papers in which the corresponding Nusselt number is defined with respect to the diameter of the cylinders. The wall condition has only a slight influence on the limiting Sherwood number. The influence of the geometry of the arrangement is much more important, triangular arrays clearly lead to higher Sherwood numbers and thus to higher mass-transfer coefficients for a given hydraulic diameter and a given fiber volume fraction.

texts.orig: The ratio between the liquid-side and gas-side Reynolds and Graetz numbers depends on the ratio between the superficial velocities of the two phases as well as the physico-chemical and geometrical system-characteristics. The corresponding expressions are presented in Table 4. It is worth noting that these relations are not dependent on either the external fiber radius or membrane permeability. Because the liquid-to-gas superficial velocity ratio is fixed by the operational constraints (the actual value is 0.00459), the ratio between the hydrodynamic characteristics of the two phases can easily be computed. It turns out that the liquid-side Reynolds numbers for the liquid-in and the liquid-out configurations are at least 4 and 75 times lower, respectively, than the gas-side Reynolds numbers. These conditions lead to laminar liquid flow over the entire investigated parameter range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The liquid-side Graetz numbers are also significantly lower than the gas-side Graetz numbers. For the two liquid flow configurations (i.e., liquid-in and liquid-out), the Graetz number at the flow-outlet section is 0.2 for 10 -3 m radius fibers and 0.4 for 2 × 10 -4 m radius fibers. These values are at least seven times greater than the limiting Graetz number that is necessary to consider a constant local Sherwood number in the estimation of the liquid-side mass-transfer coefficient (i.e., 0.03; see Table 4). Thus, the calculation procedure underestimates the local liquid-side mass-transfer coefficient in a relatively limited liquid entry section. Moreover, the assumption of a constant liquid-side mass-transfer coefficient is cautious, as it leads to a slight underestimation of the overall mass-transfer conductance in the entry section.

table_cells.text: The gas phase is a mixture of saturated air (H 2 O+N 2 +O 2 ) and carbon dioxide. The liquid phase is an aqueous solution of MEA.
The overall volumetric flow of the saturated air (H 2 O+N 2 +O 2 ) varies with the gas pressure following the ideal gas law. The overall volumetric flow of the liquid phase is constant.
The gas and the liquid are in perfect plug flow. The flow distribution is uniform, maldistribution effects are neglected.
The system is at steady state. Only counter-current operation is envisioned. Both, the external and the internal liquid-flow configuration are discussed.

Only hydrophobic porous membranes are considered, resulting in gas diffusion through the membrane. The gas-liquid interface is thus always located on the liquid-flow side of the fiber bundle. Pore size and wetting characteristics are uniform throughout the membrane. No solute transport occurs through the non-porous parts of the membrane.
Equilibrium exists at the gas-liquid interface and is described by Henry's law. The Henry constant is unvarying over the concentration range of interest.

The curvature of the gas-liquid interface does not significantly affect the rate of mass transfer or the interfacial area. The mass transfer is described adequately by simple film-type mass transfer coefficients. The geometrical internal or external membrane area is used as the gas-liquid interfacial area through which the transport takes place [4].
